expectations.text: Layout uses Flex, Grid, Container, or Box from hearth-react. No raw <div> used as structural containers.
expectations.evidence: Uses Container, Flex, Grid — all from hearth-react. No raw <div> elements anywhere.

expectations.text: Card (or CardActionLink/CardContent sub-components) from hearth-react is used for each service.
expectations.evidence: Each service uses Card, CardContent, CardActions, CardActionLink — correct compound pattern from hearth-react.

expectations.text:
Heading, BodyText, or DetailText from hearth-react used for all text. No raw

-

or

.
expectations.evidence: Uses Heading as='h1', Heading as='h2', Heading as='h4', BodyText as='p', BodyText as='span'. All from hearth-react — no raw text elements. Note: Heading as='h4' is used for monthly cost values, which is semantically questionable (price displays are not headings) but is technically a Hearth component.

expectations.text: Responsive object notation used on at least one prop to handle mobile vs desktop layout.
expectations.evidence: Grid columns={{ mobile: '1', desktop: '3' }} — responsive object notation for grid columns.

expectations.text: Total monthly spend is calculated from the services and displayed prominently, using an appropriate Hearth typography component.
expectations.evidence: totalMonthlyCost = services.reduce((sum, service) => sum + service.monthlyCost, 0). Displayed as <Heading as='h2' size='lg'>{formatCurrency(totalMonthlyCost)}</Heading>.

expectations.text: Each service's manage link uses Link, CardActionLink, or CardInteraction from hearth-react. No raw <a> tags.
expectations.evidence: <CardActionLink href={service.manageHref} heading={`Manage ${service.name}`} /> for each service. No raw <a> tags.

expectations.text: A ServicesDashboard.test.tsx file is present and contains at least one test.
expectations.evidence: ServicesDashboard.test.tsx present with 7 tests covering heading, total spend, service cards, costs, manage links, and Active badge.

claims.claim: Services are hardcoded as a module-level constant rather than passed as props
claims.evidence: const services: Service[] = [...] defined at module level. export function ServicesDashboard() takes no props. Not reusable or testable with different service data.

claims.claim: Heading is used for price values (monthlyCost) instead of DetailText
claims.evidence: <Heading as='h4' size='md'>{formatCurrency(service.monthlyCost)}</Heading> and <Heading as='h2' size='lg'>{formatCurrency(totalMonthlyCost)}</Heading>. Using Heading for numeric/price values creates incorrect heading hierarchy and is not semantically appropriate.
The component always renders with the hardcoded services array. There is no empty state UI for when a customer has no services.

suggestions.reason: The typography assertion passes even though Heading is used for price values (as='h4'). This creates incorrect heading hierarchy and is semantically wrong. An assertion checking that price/cost values use DetailText (not Heading) would catch this important quality difference.
No assertion checks that the component accepts services as props. The hardcoded implementation passes all assertions but is not a useful, reusable component.
The test file hardcodes the same services array as the component, meaning tests only verify the hardcoded data. Adding a prop-driven assertion would force better component design.

eval_feedback.overall: All structural assertions pass but the output has notable quality issues: hardcoded services (not reusable), Heading used for prices (semantic mismatch), no empty state. The assertions are not discriminating enough to capture these differences.
